Summary: NEUROD1 is a transcription factor and an essential mediator of neuronal fate specification and neuronal differentiation in multiple species. Ectopic NEUROD1 induction in pluripotent mouse ES cells overrides the pluripotent state and promotes neurogenesis. NEUROD1 directly binds promoters and enhancers of neuronal developmental genes. Chromatin immunoprecipitation in mouse E13.5 cortex confirmed NEUROD1 binding at regulatory elements of Pou3f2, Insm1, Nhlh1, Atoh1, Neurod4, and Nhlh2. Despite widespread NEUROD1 expression in the mouse peripheral nervous system, Neurod1 deletion produces substantial neurogenesis defects mainly in sensory neurons of the vestibular and spiral ganglia. In the inner ear NEUROD1 is essential for initiation of neurogenesis, delamination of neuroblasts, and survival of early neurons. In the olfactory epithelium NEUROD1 elimination spares initial olfactory sensory neuron differentiation but compromises production and survival of mature neurons. In the retina NEUROD1 deficiency causes progressive rod and cone photoreceptor degeneration and delays amacrine cell differentiation. Limited effects elsewhere may reflect redundancy with other bHLH factors including NEUROD2, NEUROD4, NSCL1/NHLH1, and NEUROG2. NEUROD1 was initially characterized in Xenopus embryos for converting nonneural ectodermal cells into neurons. Homologs in Caenorhabditis elegans, Drosophila, and mice are critical regulators of neuronal progenitor cell differentiation and neuronal specification.
References: Pavlinkova, G., & Smolik, O. (2024). NEUROD1: transcriptional and epigenetic regulator of human and mouse neuronal and endocrine cell lineage programs. Front Cell Dev Biol, 12, 1435546. https://doi.org/10.3389/fcell.2024.1435546
